assertions.text: Null input is represented as a blank cell (not the string 'null') in the table
The empty string / exception case is handled — either via a Throws? column, assertThrows inside the method, or a separate @TableTest — rather than being silently omitted
There is a result column typed as LocalDate (or using a string representation that maps to LocalDate)
assertions.evidence: void parsesDate(String input, LocalDate expected) — the 'Parsed date?' column maps to the LocalDate parameter

assertions.text: The response addresses type conversion for non-standard date formats (slash format, short year) — mentions @TypeConverter or a converter method
assertions.evidence: no `@TypeConverter` is needed here ... JUnit's built-in converter handles ISO `LocalDate` values.

assertions.text: Test method has either a @DisplayName annotation or a method name that reads as a clear, descriptive title when converted from camelCase/snake_case (e.g. 'dateParsing' → 'Date Parsing'). Not a generic name like 'test1' or 'testMethod'.
assertions.evidence: void parsesDate(String input, LocalDate expected) — 'parsesDate' reads as 'Parses Date', which is a clear descriptive title

assertions.text: @Description is present and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as supported date formats, where/when the rule applies, or open questions. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
assertions.evidence: No @Description annotation appears anywhere in the code sample. The annotations used are only @TableTest and @Test.

assertions.text: Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
assertions.evidence: Only @TableTest is present on the parsesDate method — neither @DisplayName nor @Description appear, so there is no ordering violation.

assertions.text: @Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
assertions.evidence: No @Description annotation is present in the response at all, so this requirement cannot be satisfied.
